question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() runs synchronously before SpringApplication.run() and any exception causes only a warning print, allowing the application to start with a tmpDir that is actually a file (not a directory), causing EVM contract state writes to fail silently and fund transfer results to be incorrect?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Boot auto-configuration in Web3Application to load a RuntimeHintsConfiguration override that disables reflection hints for fund-related classes, causing the EVM executor to fail to deserialize contract call results and return empty responses for fund-transferring calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @Import(CommonConfiguration.class) in Web3Application to cause CommonProperties (shard/realm) to be misconfigured, causing buildContractCallTransactionBody() to construct a ContractID with wrong shard/realm values that routes fund transfers to the wrong contract?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() does not set restrictive permissions on the newly created tmpDir (Files.createDirectories uses default umask), allowing other local users to write malicious EVM state files that affect fund transfer simulation results?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Boot application context loaded by Web3Application to trigger a lazy bean initialization race condition in TransactionExecutorFactory, causing two concurrent fund-transferring contract calls to share the same executor instance and corrupt each other's transaction state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @EnableAsync annotation in Web3Application to cause the ContractCallContext.run() lambda to execute in a thread that has inherited a stale ContractCallContext from a previous fund-transferring call, causing the new call to use the previous call's value parameter?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() does not handle the case where tmpDir is a mount point, causing FileSystemUtils.deleteRecursively() to unmount or corrupt a filesystem that stores EVM account state used for fund balance verification?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Boot auto-configuration in Web3Application to load a JacksonConfiguration that enables polymorphic deserialization, allowing a crafted JSON payload in a contract call request to instantiate arbitrary classes that manipulate the value field of fund-transferring calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @SpringBootApplication auto-configuration to load a MetricsFilter override that causes the gas used metric to be double-counted, leading ThrottleManager to under-restore gas and causing legitimate fund-transferring calls to be throttled out?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() does not validate the tmpDir path against a whitelist of allowed directories, allowing environment variable injection (e.g., via JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS) to set java.io.tmpdir to a path that overlaps with the EVM state database, causing data corruption that affects fund transfer results?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Boot property source loading in Web3Application to inject a crafted bootstrap.properties that overrides the database connection used by AccountReadableKVState, causing fund-transferring calls to read account balances from an attacker-controlled database?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @EnableAsync configuration in Web3Application to cause the restoreGasToBucket() method to be called from a different thread than the one that consumed the gas, causing a race condition in ThrottleManager that allows more gas to be restored than consumed and enabling unlimited fund-draining contract calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() does not check if the process has write permissions to tmpDir before attempting to delete it, causing an IOException that is silently swallowed, leaving stale EVM state that causes fund transfer simulations to return incorrect historical balance data?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Boot auto-configuration in Web3Application to load a ThrottleConfiguration that sets the gas bucket size to Long.MAX_VALUE, effectively disabling gas throttling and allowing unlimited fund-draining contract calls to be submitted?
